quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nuclear fission"?

options.text: Intensity I ∝ A²; for spherical waves: I = P/(4πr²), intensity decreases as 1/r²
A heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei + 2-3 neutrons + energy; ²³⁵U + n → ¹⁴¹Ba + ⁹²Kr + 3n + ~200 MeV
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nuclear fission" is defined as: A heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei + 2-3 neutrons + energy; ²³⁵U + n → ¹⁴¹Ba + ⁹²Kr + 3n + ~200 MeV. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chain reaction"?

options.text: Fission neutrons trigger more fissions; critical mass: self-sustaining; supercritical: explosive; moderated in reactors
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)
β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chain reaction" is defined as: Fission neutrons trigger more fissions; critical mass: self-sustaining; supercritical: explosive; moderated in reactors. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nuclear fusion"?

options.text: f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images
r = mv/(qB); radius of circular path depends on mass, speed, and charge-to-field ratio; the basis for particle accelerators
V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nuclear fusion" is defined as: Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Binding energy per nucleon curve"?

options.text: Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B
Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Binding energy per nucleon curve" is defined as: Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conservation laws in nuclear reactions"?

options.text: High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses
Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
When a driving frequency matches an object's natural frequency, the amplitude of oscillation increases dramatically

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conservation laws in nuclear reactions" is defined as: Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei + 2-3 neutrons + energy; ²³⁵U + n → ¹⁴¹Ba + ⁹²Kr + 3n + ~200 MeV"?

options.text: Beats
Beta-minus decay
Magnetic domains
Nuclear fission

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nuclear fission".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Fission neutrons trigger more fissions; critical mass: self-sustaining; supercritical: explosive; moderated in reactors"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Nuclear medicine
Speed of sound
Chain reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chain reaction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier"?

options.text: Nuclear fusion
Continuity equation
Wave interference
Longitudinal wave

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nuclear fusion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission"?

options.text: Reflecting telescope
Continuity equation
Doppler effect
Binding energy per nucleon curve

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Binding energy per nucleon curve".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved"?

options.text: Conservation laws in nuclear reactions
Huygens' principle
Wave energy
Rydberg formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conservation laws in nuclear reactions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Nuclear Reactions is accurate?

options.text: Electric Circuits involve the flow of charge through conductive paths driven by a potential difference.
The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
The dielectric reduces the electric field between plates and allows more charge to be stored at the same voltage.
Nuclear Reactions involve the transformation of nuclei through bombardment or collision.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Reactions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: Conservation laws apply: total charge (Z), total mass number (A), total energy, and total momentum are conserved.
The wave equation ∂²y/∂t² = v²(∂²y/∂x²) describes wave propagation in one dimension.
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Diamagnetic materials (copper, bismuth) are weakly repelled by fields—induced dipoles oppose the external field.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Reactions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: Fission: a heavy nucleus (e.g., ²³⁵U) splits into lighter nuclei + neutrons + energy.
Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Reactions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Fusion: light nuclei (e.g., ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n) combine to form a heavier nucleus + energy.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Reactions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: Photons and Quantum Mechanics introduces the quantized nature of light and energy.
Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating objects.
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Reactions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions
Electric field therapy (tumor treating fields): alternating electric fields disrupt cancer cell division—a novel application of electrostatics in medicine
Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity
Stellar composition: absorption lines in starlight reveal which elements are in the star's atmosphere; helium was first discovered in the Sun's spectrum before being found on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Nuclear Reactions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data
Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action
Fusion research: tokamak reactors (ITER) confine plasma at 150 million °C using magnetic fields to achieve controlled fusion—potentially limitless clean energy
Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Nuclear Reactions?

options.text: Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions
Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Nuclear Reactions in practice?

options.text: Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Prescription eyeglasses: optometrists measure required correction in diopters; a -3.0 D prescription means a diverging lens with f = -0.33 m for myopia correction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Reactions.
